skill.name: design-taste-frontend-v1
skill.description: Preserved v1 frontend design guidance for building distinctive React and Next.js interfaces with strong layout, motion, and polish rules.
skill.summary: Guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code toward polished, non-generic frontend UI work.
skill.author: leonxlnx

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no executable code, scripts, shell execution, cryptographic logic, or data exfiltration. The detections are false positives from Markdown inline code, CSS literals, package names, and one documented placeholder image URL.

low_findings.title: False Positive External Command Detections
low_findings.description: The static external command findings are Markdown inline examples and framework guidance, such as package names and npm install text. No shell script, executable file, or runtime command invocation exists in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Markdown instruction document and the flagged text appears inside prose or inline code formatting. I found no executable source file or command dispatch path.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Detections
low_findings.description: The high-risk blocker findings correspond to design vocabulary, numeric design settings, CSS color literals, and UI checklist text. No cryptographic API, hashing operation, credential handling, or system reconnaissance behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review shows the flagged lines describe frontend design rules and CSS values. There is no code capable of cryptographic or reconnaissance behavior.

low_findings.title: Benign Hardcoded Placeholder URL
low_findings.description: The only network finding is a recommendation to use a stable placeholder image URL format instead of broken Unsplash links. The skill does not include code that fetches this URL or sends user data to it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears in guidance text for image placeholders, not in executable network code. No evidence found of data transfer, tracking, or exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Generic AI frontend output often repeats centered heroes, default cards, and weak motion. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code concrete design rules for React and Next.js interfaces.
actual_capabilities: Defines design variance, motion intensity, and visual density settings for generated interfaces.
Guides React, Next.js, Tailwind CSS, and Server Component decisions.
Requires dependency checks before suggesting third-party frontend libraries.
Provides typography, color, layout, interaction, and performance guardrails.
Lists advanced UI concepts for navigation, grids, cards, scroll effects, and motion.
Includes a final checklist for responsiveness, cleanup, states, and animation isolation.
limitations: This is the preserved v1 skill and may differ from the current experimental version.
It is an instruction file, not a runnable frontend package or component library.
Its design opinions are strict and may conflict with an existing brand system.
Generated code still needs project-specific testing for accessibility and performance.

use_cases.title: Refresh a Product Dashboard
use_cases.description: Apply sharper spacing, typography, density, and state guidance to an existing SaaS dashboard.

use_cases.title: Generate a React Interface
use_cases.description: Use structured rules for React, Next.js, Tailwind CSS, icons, and motion choices.

use_cases.title: Improve AI Design Output
use_cases.description: Reduce repetitive AI layouts by adding explicit constraints for color, hierarchy, and interaction quality.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Polished Screen
prompt_templates.prompt: Use design-taste-frontend-v1 to design a polished React screen for a project management app. Keep motion moderate and include loading, empty, and error states.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Existing UI
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this existing Next.js page with design-taste-frontend-v1. Refine layout, spacing, typography, color, and mobile behavior without changing product features.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Interactive Dashboard
prompt_templates.prompt: Use design-taste-frontend-v1 to create a dense SaaS dashboard. Verify dependencies, isolate animated client components, and avoid generic card-heavy layout.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Motion System
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply design-taste-frontend-v1 to specify a motion system for a React app. Include component boundaries, performance rules, and reduced-motion considerations.

output_examples.input: Design a landing page for a finance analytics product.
output_examples.output: A left-aligned hero with restrained accent color, realistic data examples, responsive sections, and clear states for interactive inputs.

output_examples.input: Improve this dashboard generated by an AI assistant.
output_examples.output: A tighter information layout with fewer generic cards, clearer typography, calibrated density, and component-level motion guidance.

output_examples.input: Add motion to a feature grid without hurting performance.
output_examples.output: A plan that isolates animation components, uses transform and opacity, avoids scroll listeners, and includes cleanup expectations.

best_practices: Use the skill after sharing your project stack, installed dependencies, and brand constraints.
Ask for mobile, loading, empty, and error states in the same prompt as the main UI.
Treat the output as design guidance, then test accessibility, responsiveness, and performance in the app.
anti_patterns: Do not apply every advanced motion idea to one screen without a product reason.
Do not ignore existing design systems or brand rules just because the skill is opinionated.
Do not skip dependency verification before using Framer Motion, icon libraries, GSAP, or Three.js.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It gives AI assistants detailed frontend design rules for more polished React and Next.js interfaces.

faq.question: Is this a component library?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown instruction skill, not a package with reusable components.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it execute commands?
faq.answer: No evidence found. It only tells the assistant to verify dependencies and mention install commands when needed.

faq.question: Why is it labeled v1?
faq.answer: The skill description says this version is preserved for projects that need exact backward compatibility.

faq.question: What should users review after generation?
faq.answer: Users should review accessibility, mobile layout, performance, dependency availability, and fit with their design system.
